summary:
The Spanish Inquisition (established 1478, formally abolished 1834) was a tribunal of the Catholic Church established by the Spanish monarchs Ferdinand and Isabella under a papal bull from Pope Sixtus IV, originally to investigate and punish heresy among conversos — Jewish and Muslim converts to Christianity who were suspected of secretly maintaining their original religious practices. Unlike earlier medieval inquisitions operating under direct papal authority, the Spanish Inquisition was under royal control, functioning as an instrument of both religious orthodoxy and monarchical consolidation in the newly united Spanish kingdom.

The Inquisition's methods — arrest on secret denunciation, judicial torture to obtain confession, public trials culminating in the auto-da-fé (act of faith) where convicted heretics were publicly sentenced — created a climate of fear and suspicion, particularly among the converso population whose New Christian status was perpetually questioned. The most notorious Inquisitor General was Tomás de Torquemada (1483–1498), under whose direction thousands were tried and perhaps 2,000 executed — a number that was long exaggerated in Protestant polemical literature but represents real suffering nonetheless. The Inquisition's activities against Protestants in the 16th century and alleged witches extended its reach. The expulsion of the Jews from Spain in 1492 — the Alhambra Decree, issued the same year Columbus sailed — was a related but separate royal policy that drove hundreds of thousands of Jews into the Ottoman Empire, North Africa, and elsewhere. The Spanish Inquisition became the defining symbol in Protestant countries of Catholic religious tyranny and has profoundly shaped Western historical memory.
